purpose: Section-specific generate prompt for authoring a principle's problem field (description + examples). Composed at runtime with system_prompts/generate.json.
system_addendum:
SECTION YOU ARE AUTHORING: problem

The problem field grounds the principle in the concrete failure mode it prevents. The statement field (already authored) named the rule; this field names what breaks when the rule is absent. Solution, gates, and explain_prompt all build on the failure mode you name here — so be precise about WHICH failure this principle uniquely prevents, not a generic 'things go wrong'.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD PROBLEM

A principle's problem is { description: string, examples: string[] }.

The description is one paragraph (typically 3-6 sentences) that names the specific failure mode the principle exists to prevent AND explains the mechanism — how the absence of the principle creates the failure. The reader should be able to answer 'what breaks if this principle is absent, and why?' from the description alone. End the description with a single sentence signposting that the examples are illustrative narratives (synthesised scenarios, not documented incidents) if that is the case — the catalogue's `illustrative_examples_signposting` convention requires this when invented precision could be mistaken for cited evidence.

The examples are an array of 3-5 concrete failure patterns. Each example is one sentence (occasionally two) naming a specific scenario with named artefacts, roles, or failure patterns. Each example must demonstrate a failure that THIS principle uniquely catches — a failure that simpler controls (aggregate accuracy checks, basic monitoring, output-equality tests) or sibling principles would miss. If an example could be caught by something dumber than this principle, it accidentally argues the principle is unnecessary.

DIMENSIONS YOUR DRAFT WILL BE SCORED ON

Full rubric in sections/problem/rubric.json. The six dimensions:

1. names_failure_mode — Description names the SPECIFIC failure this principle prevents, not a generic framing.
2. failure_mechanism_explicit — Description explains HOW the absence of the principle creates the failure.
3. examples_are_concrete — Each example is specific: named artefacts, roles, observable patterns.
4. examples_distinguish_from_siblings — Each example shows a failure this principle uniquely catches; not catchable by simpler controls.
5. illustrative_signposting — If examples are invented narratives, description signposts that fact. If examples are documented incidents, no signpost needed.
6. parallel_form_with_siblings — Description voice + examples count (3-5) + example length match the siblings.

All six must score ≥ 2 to pass. Draft with these in mind from the start.

COMMON FAILURE MODES TO AVOID

- Generic failure framing ('the agent performs poorly in production', 'data quality issues degrade outcomes'). Scores low on names_failure_mode. Name the SPECIFIC mechanism this principle exists to prevent.
- Examples a sibling principle's gates would catch. E.g. when authoring problem for the drift-monitor principle, an example about 'no harness in the repo' is on the wrong principle. Scores low on examples_distinguish_from_siblings.
- Examples that any aggregate metric would catch ('accuracy dropped from 92% to 81%'). If basic monitoring catches it, this principle isn't earning its place. Use sub-threshold or hidden failures the principle uniquely surfaces.
- Missing illustrative signpost when examples are invented. Reader cannot distinguish 'this happened' from 'this is the kind of thing that happens'. Always signpost when narratives are invented.
- Wrong example count. Catalogue convention is 3-5. Fewer feels thin; more feels padded.
user_template:
You are authoring the problem field for the principle with these properties:

<principle_id>{{ principle_id }}</principle_id>
<pillar>{{ pillar }}</pillar>
<focus_area>{{ focus_area }}</focus_area>

<aws_anchor>
  <best_practice>{{ aws_bp }}</best_practice>
  <implementation_step>
    <number>{{ aws_step_number }}</number>
    <title>{{ aws_step_title }}</title>
    <verbatim_text>{{ aws_step_verbatim }}</verbatim_text>
  </implementation_step>
</aws_anchor>

<statement>
  <title>{{ statement_title }}</title>
  <description>{{ statement_description }}</description>
</statement>

<siblings_under_same_bp>
{{ sibling_problems }}
</siblings_under_same_bp>

Draft the problem (description + examples) for this principle. Return the JSON object specified in the output contract.

schema.examples: string[] — 3-5 concrete failure pattern examples, one sentence each (occasionally two)

example_passing_output.description: Without a version-controlled ground-truth harness that can be re-run on every code or configuration change to the agent, changes ship to production with unknown regression risk. There is no mechanical way to block a bad migration — only post-deployment discovery via customer impact. The harness must be part of the workload repository so every developer can run it, every CI build invokes it, and no architect's local notebook becomes a single point of failure.
examples: Original architect hand-validated production scenarios on their laptop and ran them ad-hoc; the harness was never committed to the repository; when the architect left, no one could re-run it on the next change.
Harness exists in the repository but only captures inputs, not expected outputs; CI can run the agent against each scenario but has nothing to compare its decisions against.

Harness captures the expected decision but not the expected evidence trace; an agent that stops consulting a key signal still produces the right output for the wrong reason, and the regression is invisible.
Harness entries have inconsistent free-form schema; the CI validator silently skips malformed entries, so coverage looks complete but isn't.
